sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Each action moves the white character 3 cells in that direction. - White char now at rows 14-28, cols 14-28. Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 38 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The SPACE action was marked as `no_impact: true`, meaning it only changed the HUD band, not the gameplay. But the board did change (the character rotated). Let me check the green count to see if it actually changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green total: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in greens)} in {len(greens)} pieces")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

id=2 color=c pixels=16 rows=0-0 cols=32-47

id=4 color=W pixels=135 rows=29-43 cols=41-55
id=5 color=R pixels=36 rows=32-43 cols=38-40
id=7 color=N pixels=9 rows=35-37 cols=41-43
id=9 color=N pixels=9 rows=35-37 cols=47-49
id=10 color=N pixels=54 rows=41-49 cols=41-49

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green went from 54 to 90 - it increased again! So the SPACE was a wasted move. The pattern is clear:
- SPACE toggles between two states: one reduces green, one increases it
- I need to only press SPACE when it will reduce green
Looking at the history:
- 144 -> 135 (UP, reduced)
- 135 -> 99 (UP, reduced)
- 99 -> 54 (UP, reduced)
- 54 -> 90 (DOWN, increased)
- 90 -> 99 (DOWN, increased)
- 99 -> 72 (DOWN, reduced)
- 72 -> 90 (DOWN, increased)
- 90 -> 45 (SPACE, reduced)
- 45 -> 90 (SPACE, increased)
- 90 -> 72 (UP, UP, SPACE, reduced)
- 72 -> 108 (SPACE, increased)
- 108 -> 54 (DOWN, DOWN, SPACE, reduced)
- 54 -> 90 (UP, SPACE, increased)
So the pattern is: every other SPACE reduces green. It's like SPACE has two phases: reduce and increase, alternating.
But wait, looking more carefully:
- After moving, SPACE reduced green (90 -> 45, 90 -> 72, 108 -> 54)
- After that, pressing SPACE again increased it (45 -> 90, 72 -> 108, 54 -> 90)
So the pattern is: move + SPACE = reduce, then SPACE alone = increase.
The key is: after a SPACE that reduces green, I need to move the character before pressing SPACE again.
Let me try: move character, then SPACE. The character is at rows 29-43, cols 41-55. Green pieces are:
- rows 29-31, cols 41-46 (18 pixels) - top
- rows 35-37, cols 41-43 (9 pixels) - middle left
- rows 35-37, cols 47-49 (9 pixels) - middle right
- rows 41-49 (54 pixels) - bottom
Total: 90 pixels
I need to position the character to overlap with the remaining green, then SPACE.
The bottom green (41-49) is large. Let me try DOWN to cover it, then SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green went from 54 to 90 - SPACE increased it again
# Pattern confirmed: move + SPACE reduces, SPACE alone increases
# Need to move character, then SPACE
# Current char: rows 29-43, cols 41-55
# Green: top (29-31), middle (35-37), bottom (41-49)
# Let me try DOWN to cover bottom green, then SPACE
action(['DOWN','SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
